title: Biochemistry — Metabolism, Enzymes & Molecular Biology
description: Enzyme kinetics, metabolic pathways, amino acids, DNA/RNA, signal transduction, and vitamins — high-yield biochemistry for USMLE and pre-med.
subject: chemistry

cards.front: Michaelis-Menten equation: Km and Vmax interpretation
cards.back: v = Vmax[S] / (Km + [S]). Km = [S] at ½Vmax — measure of enzyme-substrate affinity (lower Km = higher affinity). Vmax = maximum reaction rate at saturating substrate. Lineweaver-Burk (double reciprocal) plot: y-intercept = 1/Vmax, x-intercept = −1/Km

cards.front: Competitive vs uncompetitive vs noncompetitive inhibition: effects on Km and Vmax
cards.back: Competitive: ↑Km, Vmax unchanged (overcome by ↑[S]). Uncompetitive: ↓Km AND ↓Vmax (inhibitor binds ES complex). Noncompetitive (mixed): ↑Km, ↓Vmax. Pure noncompetitive (Ki = Ki'): unchanged Km, ↓Vmax. Irreversible inhibition: permanently ↓Vmax

cards.front: Allosteric regulation: positive vs negative, K vs V system
cards.back: Allosteric activators: can ↑Vmax (V-system) or ↓Km (K-system). Allosteric inhibitors: reverse effects. Cooperativity: sigmoidal kinetics (Hill equation). Phosphofructokinase-1: allosteric — AMP/ADP activate; ATP/citrate inhibit. Regulatory enzymes not at equilibrium in cell

cards.front: Glycolysis: net energy yield per glucose
cards.back: Net: 2 ATP (uses 2, produces 4), 2 NADH (in cytoplasm), 2 pyruvate. Key regulated enzymes: hexokinase/glucokinase (step 1), PFK-1 (step 3, rate-limiting), pyruvate kinase (step 10). Substrate-level phosphorylation: steps 7 (1,3-BPG → 3-PG) and 10 (PEP → pyruvate)

cards.front: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC): cofactors and regulation
cards.back: Converts pyruvate → acetyl-CoA (irreversible). Cofactors: thiamine (B1, TPP), lipoic acid, FAD (B2), NAD+ (B3, niacin), CoA (B5, pantothenate) — 'Tender Loving Care For Nerds.' PDC inhibited by: ATP, NADH, acetyl-CoA (products). Activated by: AMP, NAD+, CoA. Pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase: inhibits PDC (phosphorylation)

cards.front: TCA cycle: energy yield per acetyl-CoA
cards.back: Per acetyl-CoA: 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, 1 GTP. Fully oxidized: CO2 (2 molecules). Net per glucose (2 acetyl-CoA): 6 NADH + 2 FADH2 + 2 GTP from TCA (plus 2 NADH from pyruvate dehydrogenase). NADH → 2.5 ATP; FADH2 → 1.5 ATP via oxidative phosphorylation

cards.front: Total ATP yield from one glucose: aerobic vs anaerobic
cards.back: Aerobic: ~30–32 ATP (glycolysis 2 ATP + 2 NADH cytoplasmic, PDC 2 NADH, TCA 6 NADH + 2 FADH2 + 2 GTP). Anaerobic: 2 ATP (net glycolysis only). Regeneration of NAD+ from pyruvate → lactate (lactate dehydrogenase) required for continued glycolysis in anaerobic conditions

cards.front: Electron transport chain (ETC): complexes and inhibitors
cards.back: Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase): inhibited by rotenone, amytal. Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase): no known specific inhibitor. Complex III (cytochrome bc1): inhibited by antimycin A. Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase): inhibited by cyanide, CO, H2S, azide. ATP synthase (Complex V): inhibited by oligomycin

cards.front: Gluconeogenesis: unique enzymes (not in glycolysis)
cards.back: 4 unique enzymes: Pyruvate carboxylase (pyruvate → OAA, requires biotin, activated by acetyl-CoA). PEP carboxykinase (OAA → PEP, requires GTP). Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (F1,6BP → F6P). Glucose-6-phosphatase (G6P → glucose, liver/kidney only). Site: primarily liver, also kidney cortex

cards.front: Glycogen synthesis vs glycogen phosphorylase: key enzymes
cards.back: Glycogen synthesis: glycogen synthase (UDP-glucose → glycogen, activated by glucose-6-P, inhibited by phosphorylation). Glycogen breakdown: glycogen phosphorylase (requires PLP/B6). Regulation: insulin → dephosphorylation → ↑ synthase, ↓ phosphorylase. Glucagon/epinephrine → phosphorylation → ↑ phosphorylase

cards.front: Pentose phosphate pathway: key products and uses
cards.back: Generates: NADPH (antioxidant, reductive biosynthesis, cytochrome P450) and ribose-5-phosphate (nucleotide synthesis). Oxidative phase: glucose-6-P → ribulose-5-P + 2 NADPH + CO2 (glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase, G6PD — rate-limiting). G6PD deficiency → ↓ NADPH → ↓ glutathione → hemolytic anemia with oxidative stress

cards.front: Fatty acid synthesis: NADPH requirement and location
cards.back: Occurs in cytoplasm (liver, adipose, lactating mammary gland). Requires: NADPH (from pentose phosphate pathway and malic enzyme), malonyl-CoA (acetyl-CoA carboxylase, biotin, rate-limiting step). Fatty acid synthase: adds 2C units as malonyl-CoA. Net: 8 acetyl-CoA → palmitate (C16) + 7 CO2 + 7 ATP consumed

cards.front: Beta-oxidation of fatty acids: energy yield for palmitate (C16)
cards.back: Palmitate (16 carbons) → 7 cycles of β-oxidation → 8 acetyl-CoA. Per cycle: 1 NADH + 1 FADH2. Total: 7 NADH (17.5 ATP) + 7 FADH2 (10.5 ATP) + 8 acetyl-CoA (80 ATP from TCA) − 2 ATP activation = ~106 ATP net. Odd-chain FA: last cycle yields propionyl-CoA → succinyl-CoA (requires B12 and biotin)

cards.front: Ketone body synthesis (ketogenesis): site and conditions
cards.back: Liver mitochondria only during prolonged fasting/starvation or DKA (T1DM). 2 acetyl-CoA → acetoacetate → β-hydroxybutyrate → acetone (exhaled, fruity breath in DKA). Brain can use ketone bodies as fuel after ~3 days of fasting. Excess acetyl-CoA (fasting) cannot enter TCA (↓ OAA) → shunted to ketogenesis

cards.front: Urea cycle: key enzyme and metabolic connections
cards.back: Location: liver. Rate-limiting enzyme: carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I (CPS I). Cycle: NH4+ + CO2 → carbamoyl phosphate → citrulline → argininosuccinate → arginine → urea + ornithine. Deficiency (ornithine transcarbamoylase OTC deficiency): most common urea cycle disorder → ↑ ammonia → neurological toxicity. Connected to TCA via fumarate and aspartate

cards.front: Amino acid catabolism: nitrogen disposal steps
cards.back: Step 1: Transamination — amino group transferred to α-ketoglutarate → glutamate (PLP/B6 required). Step 2: Oxidative deamination — glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH) → NH4+ + α-ketoglutarate. NH4+ → urea cycle. Carbon skeletons → gluconeogenesis or ketogenesis (glucogenic, ketogenic, or both)

cards.front: One-carbon transfer reactions: vitamins involved
cards.back: Folate (B9): transfers one-carbon units (methyl, methylene, formyl) for purine synthesis, pyrimidine synthesis (dTMP), and amino acid metabolism. B12 (cobalamin): methylcobalamin + 5-methylTHF → methionine + THF (regenerates active folate). B12 deficiency: methylmalonyl acidemia + impaired folate recycling → megaloblastic anemia

cards.front: Collagen synthesis: steps and vitamin C role
cards.back: Pre-procollagen: signal peptide cleaved. Procollagen: hydroxylation of Pro/Lys residues (requires Vitamin C + O2 + Fe2+) → crosslinks (hydroxylysylpyridinoline). Triple helix forms. Exported → propeptides cleaved → tropocollagen → collagen fibrils. Vitamin C deficiency: scurvy — ↓ hydroxylation → unstable triple helix → wound healing defect

cards.front: DNA replication: leading vs lagging strand synthesis
cards.back: Leading strand: continuous synthesis 5'→3' from one primer. Lagging strand: discontinuous — Okazaki fragments synthesized 5'→3' (away from replication fork). DNA ligase seals fragments. Helicase unwinds. Primase synthesizes RNA primer. DNA Pol III (prokaryote)/Pol δ (eukaryote): elongation. Pol I: removes RNA primer, gap fills

cards.front: DNA repair mechanisms: mismatch vs base excision vs nucleotide excision
cards.back: Mismatch repair (MMR): corrects replication errors (single base mismatches). Deficiency: Lynch syndrome (HNPCC). Base excision (BER): removes damaged/modified bases (deaminated cytosine, oxidized guanine). Nucleotide excision (NER): removes bulky lesions (pyrimidine dimers from UV). Deficiency: Xeroderma pigmentosum (↑ skin cancer)

cards.front: Transcription factors: general vs specific
cards.back: General transcription factors (GTFs): assemble at core promoter (TATA box) with RNA pol II → basal transcription. Specific/enhancer-binding factors: bind enhancers/silencers → activate/repress transcription. Signal-activated TFs: steroid hormone receptors (nuclear), CREB (cAMP), NF-κB (inflammation), Myc (cell cycle)

cards.front: Post-translational modifications (PTMs): types and functions
cards.back: Phosphorylation (Ser/Thr/Tyr): activate/inactivate enzymes (signal transduction). Glycosylation: cell recognition, protein folding, stability. Ubiquitination: proteasomal degradation. SUMOylation: nuclear transport, gene regulation. Acetylation: chromatin remodeling (histone acetylation → open chromatin = active transcription)

cards.front: cAMP signaling pathway: components
cards.back: Ligand → Gs-coupled GPCR → adenylyl cyclase activated → cAMP ↑ → PKA activated → phosphorylates targets. PDE degrades cAMP. Examples: glucagon, epinephrine (β), TSH, LH, FSH, ACTH, PTH, vasopressin (V2). Theophylline and caffeine: PDE inhibitors → ↑ cAMP. Cholera toxin: ADP-ribosylates Gs → constitutively active

cards.front: Receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) signaling: pathway
cards.back: Ligand (insulin, EGF, PDGF) → receptor dimerization → autophosphorylation → Ras activation → Raf → MEK → ERK/MAPK → transcription (proliferation, differentiation). Ras mutation (oncogene): constitutively active → cancer. Neurofibromatosis: NF1 is Ras-GAP → loss → overactive Ras signaling

cards.front: G-protein subtypes and effectors
cards.back: Gs: ↑ adenylyl cyclase → ↑ cAMP. Gi: ↓ adenylyl cyclase → ↓ cAMP (opioids, α2 adrenergic). Gq: activates PLC → IP3 (↑ Ca2+) + DAG (activates PKC). G12/13: activates Rho → cytoskeleton. Cholera: ADP-ribosylates Gs (stays on). Pertussis toxin: ADP-ribosylates Gi (stays off)

cards.front: Enzyme cofactor vitamins: summary table
cards.back: B1 (thiamine/TPP): α-keto acid decarboxylation (PDC, α-KGD), transketolase. B2 (FAD/FMN): oxidation-reduction (ETC, β-oxidation, PDC). B3 (NAD+/NADH): glycolysis, TCA, β-oxidation. B5 (CoA): acyl transfers. B6 (PLP): transamination, glycogen phosphorylase, decarboxylation. Biotin (B7): carboxylation (ACC, PC). B12: methylation, mutase reactions

cards.front: Fat-soluble vitamins: ADEK functions and toxicity
cards.back: A (retinol): vision (rhodopsin, 11-cis retinal), epithelial differentiation. D: calcium/phosphate absorption, bone mineralization. E (tocopherol): antioxidant, protects RBC membranes. K: carboxylation of clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X) and proteins C/S. Toxicity: hypervitaminosis A (teratogenic, intracranial HTN), D (hypercalcemia)

cards.front: Nucleotide synthesis: de novo vs salvage pathway
cards.back: De novo: synthesizes from scratch using amino acids, CO2, folate derivatives. Purines: PRPP + glycine/aspartate/glutamine/folate → IMP → AMP or GMP. Pyrimidines: carbamoyl phosphate + aspartate → UMP → CTP, TTP. Salvage: recycles free bases (HGPRT for purines). HGPRT deficiency: Lesch-Nyhan syndrome (↑ uric acid, self-mutilation, gout)

cards.front: Purine synthesis regulation: key enzyme
cards.back: Rate-limiting step: glutamine PRPP amidotransferase. Inhibited by AMP and GMP (product feedback inhibition). Stimulated by PRPP. PRPP synthetase: inhibited by AMP, GMP, IMP (purine ribonucleotides). Allopurinol: inhibits xanthine oxidase → ↓ uric acid production → treats gout

cards.front: RNA polymerases: types and function (eukaryotic)
cards.back: RNA Pol I: rRNA (large: 28S, 18S, 5.8S). RNA Pol II: mRNA and snRNA. RNA Pol III: tRNA, 5S rRNA, and snRNA. All require DNA template and ribonucleoside triphosphates. α-Amanitin (death cap mushroom): inhibits RNA Pol II (mRNA synthesis) at low doses, Pol III at high doses. No effect on Pol I

cards.front: CRISPR-Cas9: mechanism briefly
cards.back: Cas9 endonuclease guided by sgRNA (single guide RNA, complementary to target DNA) → Cas9 binds → cleaves double-strand DNA adjacent to PAM sequence (NGG for SpCas9) → DSB repaired by NHEJ (error-prone, causes mutations) or HDR (with donor template, precise edit). Used for gene editing, functional studies, and emerging therapeutics

cards.front: Western blot vs Southern blot vs Northern blot
cards.back: Southern: DNA detection (gel → membrane → DNA probe). Northern: RNA detection (gel → membrane → RNA probe). Western: protein detection (gel → membrane → antibody). ELISA: antibody-based protein detection in solution. 'SNoW DRoP': South = DNA, North = RNA, West = Protein

cards.front: PCR (polymerase chain reaction): steps and components
cards.back: Denaturation (94–95°C): separate strands. Annealing (50–65°C): primers bind. Extension (72°C): Taq DNA polymerase synthesizes new strand (5'→3'). Repeats exponentially (2^n cycles = 2^n copies). RT-PCR: RNA → cDNA via reverse transcriptase → then PCR. qPCR: quantifies DNA in real time with fluorescent probes

cards.front: Protein structure levels: 1°, 2°, 3°, 4°
cards.back: 1°: amino acid sequence (peptide bonds). 2°: local folding via H-bonds (α-helix, β-sheet). 3°: overall 3D shape via hydrophobic interactions, disulfide bonds, H-bonds, van der Waals. 4°: multiple polypeptide chains (quaternary) — e.g., hemoglobin (2α + 2β). Chaperones (HSP70/HSP60) assist proper folding and prevent aggregation

cards.front: Hemoglobin cooperativity: T vs R state
cards.back: Tense (T) state: low O2 affinity (deoxyHb). Relaxed (R) state: high O2 affinity (oxyHb). Cooperative binding: sigmoid O2 dissociation curve. Right shift (↑ P50, ↓ affinity): ↑ CO2, ↑ H+, ↑ temp, ↑ 2,3-BPG (Bohr effect). HbF: ↓ 2,3-BPG binding → ↑ O2 affinity → left shift (favors O2 transfer from mother to fetus)

cards.front: Ubiquitin-proteasome pathway: mechanism
cards.back: Target protein polyubiquitinated (E1 activating → E2 conjugating → E3 ligase transfers ubiquitin to Lys on substrate). Polyubiquitinated protein → 26S proteasome (proteolytic degradation). Regulates: cell cycle (cyclin degradation), apoptosis (p53 stabilization), NF-κB activation. Defective: Parkinson disease (Lewy bodies = ubiquitin + α-synuclein aggregates)

cards.front: Oxidative stress: antioxidant systems
cards.back: Superoxide dismutase (SOD): O2•− → H2O2. Catalase: H2O2 → H2O + O2. Glutathione peroxidase: H2O2 + 2GSH → GSSG + 2H2O. Glutathione reductase: GSSG + NADPH → 2GSH (requires NADPH from pentose phosphate pathway). G6PD deficiency → ↓ NADPH → ↓ reduced glutathione → ↑ oxidative damage → hemolysis

cards.front: Citric acid cycle: anaplerotic reactions
cards.back: Anaplerosis: reactions that replenish TCA intermediates. Key: pyruvate carboxylase (pyruvate + CO2 → OAA, requires biotin) — liver and kidney. Glutamine → α-ketoglutarate. Propionyl-CoA → succinyl-CoA. Important when TCA intermediates drawn off for biosynthesis (amino acids, heme, glucose). PC most important

cards.front: Heme synthesis: steps, location, and enzyme defects
cards.back: Starts in mitochondria: ALA synthase (rate-limiting, B6 PLP, inhibited by heme). Porphobilinogen made in cytoplasm. Steps alternate mitochondria/cytoplasm. Lead poisoning: inhibits ALA dehydratase and ferrochelatase → ↑ ALA and protoporphyrin in urine/blood. Porphyrias: autosomal dominant enzyme defects → porphyrin accumulation

cards.front: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH): isoforms and clinical use
cards.back: LDH1 (heart, RBC): elevated in MI. LDH2: highest in serum normally. LDH3: lungs. LDH4: kidney, placenta. LDH5: liver, skeletal muscle. Flip (LDH1 > LDH2): consistent with myocardial infarction (before widespread troponin use). LDH also elevated in hemolysis, malignancy, megaloblastic anemia

cards.front: Cholesterol biosynthesis: rate-limiting step and statin mechanism
cards.back: Rate-limiting step: HMG-CoA reductase (converts HMG-CoA → mevalonate). Statins: competitive inhibitors of HMG-CoA reductase → ↓ cholesterol synthesis → ↑ LDL receptor expression → ↓ serum LDL. Mevalonate also precursor for: CoQ10, dolichol, geranyl/farnesyl groups (protein prenylation)

cards.front: Phospholipid synthesis: key intermediate
cards.back: CDP-choline or CDP-ethanolamine (Kennedy pathway) → phospholipid synthesis in ER. Phosphatidylcholine: most abundant membrane phospholipid. Phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate (PIP2): PLC cleaves → IP3 + DAG (second messengers). Surfactant: DPPC (dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine) — synthesized by type II pneumocytes, requires phosphatidylcholine

cards.front: Ceramide and sphingolipidoses
cards.back: Ceramide = sphingosine + fatty acid (backbone of sphingolipids). Sphingomyelin: ceramide + phosphocholine. Sphingolipidoses: enzyme deficiencies causing sphingolipid accumulation. Niemann-Pick (sphingomyelinase): cherry-red spot, hepatosplenomegaly. Gaucher (β-glucocerebrosidase): Gaucher cells (most common). Tay-Sachs (hexosaminidase A): cherry-red spot, no HSM

cards.front: Folate trap in B12 deficiency: mechanism
cards.back: B12-dependent methionine synthase: transfers methyl from methylTHF → methionine, releasing THF. B12 deficiency → methyl-THF trapped (folate trap) → ↓ active THF → ↓ dTMP synthesis → impaired DNA synthesis → megaloblastic anemia. Explains why folate supplementation partially masks B12 deficiency (corrects hematology but NOT neuro)
